---
title: "LaTeX Project — replicate a short open-access math paper"
subtitle: "Your first major portfolio piece"
toc: true
---

> **Pairs with:** the LaTeX block of the course (weeks 2–3) and the
> Week 7 LaTeX Project studio. Due date and submission link live in
> Blackboard.

## Goal

Recreate the look and structure of a short, open-access mathematics
paper (or a clearly delimited section of one, about 4–6 pages). The
focus is on **clean mathematical typesetting**: math environments,
theorem and proof blocks, cross-references, a figure or table, and a
short bibliography.

This is your first major portfolio piece. Expect it to be the most
polished thing you have written in LaTeX so far.

## What you will submit

Submit all of the following to Blackboard:

- The compiled **PDF**.
- Your **source files**: either `.tex` (with optional `.bib`) **or** a
  Quarto `.qmd` with `format: pdf`, plus any figure files.
- A short **Challenges Note** (1 page max) describing the main
  formatting issues you faced and how you addressed them.
- An **AI Use Note** if you used AI assistance — 4–8 sentences, no
  raw prompt transcripts. See
  [resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd](../resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd).

**File naming.** `Lastname_First_LaTeXProject.pdf` plus the source
file(s) and figure(s) in a folder named `Lastname_First_LaTeXProject/`.

## How to choose a source paper

Prefer **arXiv** for reliability and easy access.

1. Go to <https://arxiv.org> and choose **Mathematics**, then a
   subject like `math.PR` (Probability), `math.CA` (Classical
   Analysis), or `math.CO` (Combinatorics).
2. Search for short notes or elementary proofs. Phrases like
   "short note," "elementary proof," or topic terms (e.g., "Chernoff
   bounds," "Borel–Cantelli," "Lipschitz existence") work well.
3. Pick a PDF that is **4–8 pages**, with **at least two displayed
   equations**, **one theorem/proof**, and ideally a figure or table.
4. Avoid manuscripts whose argument depends on prior sections that
   aren't included.

**Get my sign-off** on your chosen paper at the Week 7 studio meeting
(or via Blackboard message earlier). This is the one checkpoint that
catches misjudged scope.

If you'd rather use an open-access paper from a journal (e.g., AMS
Notices, MAA Monthly, an OA mathematics journal), that's fine.
Check with me first.

## Project requirements

Each item lists points in brackets.

1. **Structure.** Title and author block, abstract, sectioning, page
   numbers, sensible margins. **[20]**
2. **Math environments.** Use `amsmath`. Include at least **two
   displayed equations** and at least **one `align`-style
   environment**. Define **theorem and proof environments** (via
   `amsthm` or equivalent). **[20]**
3. **Figures or tables.** Include **at least one** with a caption
   and label; reference it in the text. **[10]**
4. **Bibliography.** Use `biblatex` or `natbib` (your choice) with at
   least **three entries**. Cite each in the text. **[15]**
5. **Polish.** Consistent notation, consistent spacing, no overfull
   boxes. Display equations follow a consistent convention (e.g.,
   period at the end if that's your style). **[20]**
6. **Challenges Note.** A concise discussion of the main obstacles
   and how you fixed them. Not a journal — just the real friction.
   **[15]**

**Compilation checklist (–5 if broken):**

- Your project compiles **from a clean folder** with one command
  sequence (e.g., `pdflatex` → `biber` → `pdflatex` ×2, or
  `quarto render` for a Quarto source).
- All paths are **relative** (no absolute paths to your desktop).

Total: 100 (with a possible 5-point penalty for broken builds).

## Two acceptable workflows

You can use either of these — pick what feels right for the paper
you're replicating.

**A. Pure `.tex` source.**
Standard `\documentclass{article}` with `amsmath`, `amsthm`,
`graphicx`, `biblatex`. Build with `pdflatex` + `biber`.

**B. Quarto with `format: pdf`.**
A `.qmd` file using LaTeX math syntax (`$$...$$`), with a
bibliography via Pandoc citation processing. Build with
`quarto render`. This option lets you reuse what you've been writing
all term in a Quarto-first style.

Both are accepted. Pick one and stick with it.

## AI use (optional but encouraged)

You may use AI to help with LaTeX syntax, package suggestions, and
debugging. If you do:

- Include an **AI Use Note** (4–8 sentences) describing what you
  asked for, what worked, what didn't, and how you verified the result.
- Do **not** paste long prompt transcripts. Summarize.
- You remain responsible for **correctness**, **reproducibility**, and
  **citation quality**.

Common AI pitfalls to watch for:

- **Hallucinated packages** that don't exist on CTAN.
- **Hallucinated `\bibitem` entries** or invented citation keys.
- **Wrong environment names** (e.g., suggesting `theorem` syntax that
  doesn't actually compile with the package you're using).
- **Wrong cross-reference syntax** for the bibliography style you
  loaded.

When in doubt: compile early and often, and copy the **actual error
message** (not a paraphrase) into your next AI prompt.

## A note on academic integrity

You are **replicating the look and structure** of a paper, not
plagiarizing its content. Make this transparent in your Challenges
Note ("I replicated the structure of [author, year, title] as a
typesetting exercise; the mathematical content is taken from the
original and cited.").
